coredata.dc:title: Novel carboxylic functionalized ionic liquid grafted on carbonized polymer dots as highly efficient corrosion inhibitors

coredata.dc:description: A one-step hydrothermal synthesis of ionic liquid grafted carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) from l-cysteine and carboxylic acid functionalized ionic liquid is presented. The obtained CPDs have been characterized by various methods, found multiples functional groups have been detected on the CPD surfaces from FT-IR and XPS and 13C NMR, and ionic liquid serves as both the reaction medium and the reaction precursor for the grafting, endows a charged surface and can prevent the aggregation of carbon dots, whose sizes are around 100 nm from HRTEM. The CPDs exhibit excellent corrosion inhibition efficiency of 94.07 % to carbon steel at 50 mg·L-1 in 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution, and low surface roughness can be observed on the inhibited steel surface by SEM, and a mixed adsorption of CPD on steel surface, including chemisorption and physisorption, can be demonstrated by XPS, and finally molecular dynamic study shows that the parallel adsorption mode is preferred to ensure nice inhibiting efficiency. This work provides a promising preparation strategy for CPDs, with ionic liquid grafted, for effective corrosion inhibition. which ensures multiple surface adsorption sites.

head.ce:title: Novel carboxylic functionalized ionic liquid grafted on carbonized polymer dots as highly efficient corrosion inhibitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A one-step hydrothermal synthesis of ionic liquid grafted carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) from l-cysteine and carboxylic acid functionalized ionic liquid is presented. The obtained CPDs have been characterized by various methods, found multiples functional groups have been detected on the CPD surfaces from FT-IR and XPS and 13C NMR, and ionic liquid serves as both the reaction medium and the reaction precursor for the grafting, endows a charged surface and can prevent the aggregation of carbon dots, whose sizes are around 100 nm from HRTEM. The CPDs exhibit excellent corrosion inhibition efficiency of 94.07 % to carbon steel at 50 mg·L-1 in 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution, and low surface roughness can be observed on the inhibited steel surface by SEM, and a mixed adsorption of CPD on steel surface, including chemisorption and physisorption, can be demonstrated by XPS, and finally molecular dynamic study shows that the parallel adsorption mode is preferred to ensure nice inhibiting efficiency. This work provides a promising preparation strategy for CPDs, with ionic liquid grafted, for effective corrosion inhibition. which ensures multiple surface adsorption sites.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal corrosion can menace industry equipment and human life [1–3]. As an economical method, the inhibitor is an effective way to prevent metal corrosion [4–7]. However, poisonous inhibitors, such as chromate and phosphorous compounds, can harm human health and ecology when they enter the environment, compelling the researcher to exploit a new inhibitor that is efficient and environmentally friendly [8–10]. As a member of the novel carbon-based functional nanomaterial, the carbon dots (CDs) are demonstrated to have significant potential as inhibitor candidates, due to their water solubility, more environmentally friendly, and excellent inhibition performance [11–16]. According to the previous publications, heteroatom doping is an effective method to improve the inhibition efficiency of carbon dots [17]. Xu et al. synthesized the N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and N, S-codoped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) through a biochemical oxidation reaction, and corrosion inhibition efficiency of N, S-CDs (91.74 %) is higher than NCDs (79.20 %) at 200 mg·L-1 in 1 mol·L-1 HCl solutions [18]. Zhang et al. confirm the S-rich carbon dots enhanced the adsorption, and the inhibition efficiency against H2SO4 attack on X65 steel can reach 99.1 % with 200 mg·L-1 of N, S-CDs [19]. Therefore, N, S-rich CDs will be an interesting topic for us to study.

According to their structure and fluorescence emission mechanisms, there are four categories of CDs, including the graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [20]. Among them, the CPDs exhibit a polymer/carbon hybrid structure, which consists of rich functional groups/polymer chains and carbon cores [21]. In the process of fabricating of CPDs, the precursor undergoes the process of condensation, crosslinking, and carbonization reaction. Abundant polymer chains and functional groups are formed on the carbon dot surface, including amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups, after incomplete carbonization [13,22,23]. The unique polymer/carbon hybrid structure of CPDs endows multiple adsorption sites and are expected to be an efficient corrosion inhibitor. However, there are few reports on CPDs for corrosion inhibition in acidic media.

The role of ILs in the fabrication of CDs has been demonstrated to be a reaction medium, catalyst, and functionalized molecule, varying in different situations according to the nature of the carbon precursors and ILs and finally affecting their physical and chemical properties [24–27]. As we know, functionalized ionic liquids (ILs) themself are promising corrosion inhibitors in our previous studies [28–32]. Combining the designability of ILs by functionalizing the side chains of the imidazolium ring, the derived CDs with unique characters will be obtained. Therefore, CDs derived from the functionalized ILs will be an effective strategy for enhanced inhibition efficiency.

During the CDs preparation, the ILs exhibited apparent advantages over conventional organic solvents, such as having almost no vapor pressure, excellent thermal and chemical stability, and low melting point [33–35]. Furthermore, the ILs are beneficial to the prevention of the aggregation of CDs because low vapor pressure and surface energy favor the rapid nucleation rate of CDs [24,25,36,37]. In addition, ILs functionalized CDs are stabilized without significant aggregation [25]. In our previous work, CDs corrosion inhibitor were successfully prepared without /with ionic liquid [37,38]. The CDs inhibitor, prepared from l-cysteine in the presence of ionic liquid 1‑butyl‑3-methylimidazolium bromide, showed good corrosion inhibition efficiency of 97 % at 40 mg·L-1 [38]. The abundant surface functional groups are an effective strategy to enhance the corrosion inhibition effect of CDs; therefore, the fabrication of CDs surface structure by specific reaction (e.g., amidation reaction) of ILs with l-cysteine-derived carbon dots in a facile way is highly pursued for effective corrosion retardation.

Herein, we choose carboxyl-functionalized imidazolium-based ILs, working as both reaction media and precursors, for the hydrothermal preparation of heteroatom doping (N, S-rich) CPDs from l-cysteine as precursors, and then evaluate them as corrosion inhibitors in 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4. l-cysteine molecule, with multifunctional groups, including the amino, carboxylic acid, and hydrosulphonyl groups, can react with not only other l-cysteine molecules but also the imidazolium-based ILs via its carboxylic acid group to form CPDs via dehydration, crosslinking, and carbonization reactions. This surface-engineering strategy facilitates the functional ionic liquid grafted on the surface of the carbon core via the reaction of the amino group in l-cysteine and the carboxyl group in ILs, which makes the obtained CPDs with unique characters compared with other carbon dots. The charged CPDs surface induces electrostatic repulsion among CPDs, thereby preventing the aggregation of CPDs and facilitating their good dispersion. Abundant functional groups on the CPDs surface provide multiple adsorption sites anchored on the metal surface. Finally, the rich N- & S- heteroatoms in the CPDs’ structure result in excellent inhibition performance. The corrosion inhibition of CPDs was investigated by various experimental methods and molecular dynamic calculations, and the related mechanism was discussed in detail.

The 1-Methylimidazole (C4H6N2, AR, 99 %) and 3-chloropropionic acid (C3H5ClO2, AR, 98 %) were procured from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The l-cysteine (L-cys) (C3H7NO2S, AR, 99 %) was acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. The sulfuric acid (H2SO4, AR, 98 %) was purchased from Tianjin Fengchuan Co., Ltd. The ILs for 1-carboxyethyl-3-methylimidazolium hydrogensulfate were synthesized, and the synthetic routes are shown in Fig. S1.

The carbon steel 45# with the dimensions of 10 × 10 × 2 mm was used. The chemical components of 45# steel is listed in Table S1. Before electrochemical measurement and surface characterization, the carbon steel 45# sample was polished with 180#, 600#, 1000#, 1500#, and 2000# grit sandpaper to remove corrosive production, cleaned, and used acetone rinsed, and finally dried.

The 5 g of l-cys and 2 g of 1-carboxyethyl-3-methylimidazolium hydrogensulfate were mixed uniformly in 50 mL Teflon liner for heating for 4 h at 220 °C. After cooling, the product was dissolved in 80 mL of water. Then, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min. The supernate was dialyzed for 24 h through a 1000 Da dialysis bag (change the water every 6 h). The CPDs product was obtained by vacuum drying. It can adjust the reaction temperature to 200, 180, and 160 °C to obtain the different CPDs, named CPDs-200, CPDs-180, and CPDs-160, respectively. It can adjust 1-carboxyethyl-3-methylimidazolium hydrogensulfate to 1-propyl-3-methylimidazolium hydrogensulfate, named CPDs-[PMIm]HSO4. The synthesis process is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The detailed characterizations of CPDs are described in the Supporting Information.

The electrochemical measurements were carried out at 25 °C using a traditional three-electrode system. The system consisted of 200 mL of a 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution, both blank solution and with varying concentrations of as-prepared CPDs. All electrochemical measurements were conducted in an electrochemical workstation (Gamry, USA, Reference 3000). As a working electrode (WE), the carbon steel 45#, with an exposed area of 1 cm2, was employed. The Ag/AgCl with fine luggin's capillaries and platinum foil were treated as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. All potential values are referenced to Ag/AgCl electrodes in this work. In particular, new corrosion solutions are utilized for each measurement. For electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the 45# steel was immersed in a corrosive solution for 60 min to establish stable open circuit potential (E OCP). A sinusoidal AC signal amplitude of 10 mV and a frequency range of 100 kHz to 50 mHz was collected from the EIS data. The EIS measurements start potential is stable E OCP values. Following the EIS test, the potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) was executed by a scanning rate of 0.5 mV·s-1 versus the Ag/AgCl electrode in a potential range of ± 250 mV. The electrochemical measurements carried out in static.

After immersion in a corrosive solution, we examined the morphologies and chemical constitution of 45# steel surfaces, adding CPDs by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Firstly, carbon steel 45# that was properly cleaned and dried was immersed in 50 mL 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution with 150 mg·L-1 of CPDs-220 for 2 h at 25 °C. After that, specimens were extracted from the corrosive solution, subjected to a thorough rinse with isopropyl alcohol and water, and subsequently dried. The SEM (Hitachi TM3030) described surface morphologies after corrosion. The XPS (Thermo Fisher NEXSA) analyzed the chemical constitution of 45# steel after corrosion.

We used the “Forcite” module to perform MD simulations, and the progression of CPDs inhibitor adsorption on the Fe (1 1 0) surface was examined. As a result, the densely packed Fe (1 1 0) surface model was selected as a model. The MD simulations were conducted using a COMPASSⅡ force field and periodic boundary conditions in a Fe (1 1 0) supercell (37.24 Å × 37.24 Å × 28.11 Å). The location of each Fe atom was maintained as "frozen" in its original place. All MD simulations used a simulation temperature of 298 K and an NVT canonical ensemble with a time step of 1 fs and a simulation time of 5000 ps.

Where E interaction is the energy of interaction between CPDs and Fe (1 1 0), E total represents the energy of the total simulating system, E surface denotes the energy of Fe (1 1 0) surfaces and E inhibitor is the energy of CPDs inhibitor.

We employed selected UV–vis, FT-IR, and XPS to probe the structure and chemical compositions of CPDs. The water solution of CPDs-160–220 obtained displays a transparent or pale-yellow color under daylight, while a blue fluorescence was observed in 365 nm ultra-violet light (Fig. S3). The UV−vis spectrum of CPDs (Fig. 2a ) shows the π−π* transitions of C = C and C = N bonds and conjugated structure in carbon cores at 228 and 260 nm, with each CDs, have similar adsorption peak features [39]. The distinct absorption peaks at 330 nm for CPDs-200 and CPDs-220 were observed ascribed to n−π* transitions [40], while the peak was not observed at 330 nm for CPDs-160 and CPDs-180. The FT-IR of CPDs (Fig. 2b) displays two prominent characteristic stretching vibrations of the NH and CH bond at 3216 and 2928 cm-1, respectively [41]. The peaks observed at 1648, 1534, and 1400 cm-1 were associated with -NHC = O, C = N, and CN bonds, respectively [40,42–45]. The existents of amide bonds confirm that imidazolium-based ILs containing a carboxyl group are involved in the formation of CPDs. The l-cysteine's amino groups are reacted with the carboxyl groups of the imidazolium-based ILs to form CPDs by dehydration, crosslinking, and carbonization reaction. The [HO2CEtMIm][HSO4] survived as the surface modifier of the CPDs due to its high thermal stability [46,47]. The FT-IR result suggests that the CPDs are successfully modified by the ionic liquid. The broad absorbance from 604 cm-1 was due to C-S vibrations, which confirmed S elements are successfully doped into CDs [41,48]. The presence of -SO3 in CPDs-200 and CPDs-220, as indicated by the peaks observed at 1068 cm-1, can be attributed to the oxidation of -SH group [41].

To identify whether the N and S elements have functionalized CPDs successfully, the XPS survey was carried out. The XPS spectrum (Fig. S4a) demonstrates the synthesized CPDs comprised of C, N, O, and S elements, and the spectra are of high resolution for the elements listed. The C 1 s signal of CPDs (Fig. 3a ) is decomposed into CC/C = C bonds (284.8 eV), CN/CO/C-S bonds (285.8 eV), and C = O bonds (288.2 eV) [41,49,50]. For the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3b), the peaks at 399.2 eV, 400.4 eV, and 401.2 eV confirm the presence of three N species, including pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N, and graphitic N, further indicating the N element successful doped into CPDs [36,51]. For the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3c), in which the three prominent peaks C = O, CO-C/COH, and O = CO bonds are located at 531.0 eV, 531.8 eV, and 532.9 eV, respectively [52]. For the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 3d), CPDs-200 and CPDs-220 were fitted into five peaks, while CPDs-160 and CPDs-180 were divided into two peaks. There, a double peak with the binding energies of 163.3 eV and 164.5 eV is relevant to C-S bonds [49,53–55]. The peak at 161.8 eV can be associated with S2- [38]. The two peaks (Fig. 3d) at 167.8 and 169.0 eV corresponded to the -C-SOX species [56,57]. Furthermore, after improving reaction temperature, the S 2p spectrum for CPDs-200 and CDs-220 displays differences from CPDs-160 and CPDs-180, with the primary distinction arising from the -C-SO3 species. The oxidation of -SH results in the formation of C-SO3 at 200 °C, followed by the predominant existence of sulfur as C-SO3 at a temperature of 220 °C.

The morphologies of CPDs from various reaction temperatures were visualized by HRTEM imaging. The HRTEM image (Fig. 4 ) reveals that the synthesized CPDs exhibit uniform dispersion and possess amorphous spherical morphology, leading to poor crystallinity. These CPDs exhibit specialized structures, comprising a carbon core and polymer chains. Meanwhile, many polymer chains appeared crust upon the carbon core. The average particle size of CPDs-160–220 is 79.62, 82.33, 91.56, and 103.86 nm, respectively. As the temperature rises, the degree of graphitization of the CPDs gradually increases, and the particle size gradually increases. The low reaction temperature results in a lower graphitization degree for the carbon core. The polymer chains continue carbonization with the increase of reaction temperature and result in the average CPDs particle size increase. In particular, the average particle size of CPDs-[PMIm]HSO4 is 14 nm (Fig. S17), much lower than CPDs-160–220.

The PL spectra (Fig. S5) show the four CPDs at excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 nm to 380 nm. The CPDs have excitation-determined emission fluorescence properties. For CPDs-160, CPDs-180, CPDs-200, and CPDs-220, when the excitation wavelength is 350, 340, 350, and 340 nm corresponds to the optimal fluorescence emission wavelength is 438, 422, 425, and 406 nm, respectively.

The 1H NMR (Fig. S6) and 13C NMR spectra (Fig. S7) provide the supporting information of the structure of CPDs-220. It can be seen in Fig S7 and S8, there was an obvious difference in the 13C NMR spectra between the CPDs-220 and the mixture of l-cys and [HO2CEtMIm]HSO4, which means that the CPDs-220 cannot be regards as only a mixture of the reaction precursors, and is obtained via dehydration, condensation, and carbonization reactions at a preparation temperature of 220 °C. The 13C NMR spectra of CPDs-220 reveal the presence of sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms, indicating the existence of functional groups, carbon cores, and polymer chains. The chemical shift peak at 110–120 ppm was attributed to sp2 carbon in the carbon core. The chemical shift peak at 130 ppm was ascribed to C = N bonds. Carbon polymer chains were observed on the surface of carbon dots, exhibiting a chemical shift peak at 10–40 ppm. The chemical-shift peaks at 170–180 ppm were assigned to C = O groups originating from the -COOH group and amide bonds. The NMR results are consistent with the FT-IR analyses.

TGA cures (Fig. S9) display distinct weight loss at 220 °C, indicating the decomposition of l-cys at this temperature. The TGA analysis reveals abundant functional groups exist on the surface of CPDs, which offer benefits in promoting multi-anchor adsorption onto metal surfaces.
Fig. S16 and Fig. S18 display the structure and chemical composition information of CPDs-[PMIm]HSO4. The result showed that CPDs-[PMIm]HSO4 is composed of C, N, O, and S elements.

The corrosion behaviors of 45# steel unprotected and protected with CPDs in 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution were investigated. In Fig. 5 , in the case of CPD inhibitors, there is a capacitive loop located at the high-frequency region, which is ascribed to the charge transfer process of the corrosion reaction on the steel surface [58]. The inductive phenomena observed in the low-frequency region are attributed to the adsorption or desorption of (SO4 2-) ions and CPDs on the steel surface, which induces an inductive loop [59]. The Nyquist plots exhibit a lack of a perfect semicircle, named dispersion effect, which is explained by the unevenness of the steel surface [60]. A similar feature of Nyquist plots for 45# steel protection by as-prepared CPD inhibitors was observed, indicating that CPD inhibitors only reduce the corrosion reaction rate, and the mechanism of corrosion reaction was unchanged [61]. The introduction of CPD inhibitors results in an enlargement of the capacitor loop diameter, demonstrating that CPD inhibitors exhibit satisfactory inhibition performance by restraining the charge transfer process. In addition, the diameter of the capacitor loop increases since CPD inhibitors concentrations increase. The CPDs-220 exhibits a larger diameter than that of another inhibitors, meaning it has the best inhibition efficiency.

For Bode plots (Fig. 6 ), there are prominent peaks in Bode plots corresponding to a one-time consist relevant to the corrosion reaction on steel surfaces. With the introduction and increased concentration of CPD inhibitors, the frequency area of phase angles widened, and the phase angle values rose. The concentration of CPD inhibitors increases the impedance modulus in the lowest frequency range. The more outstanding the corrosion inhibition performance, the greater the impedance modulus at the lowest frequency range [62]. These results indicated that CPD inhibitors could be adsorbed on steel surfaces to produce an adsorption layer, which hinders the electron transfer process.

The EIS data were fitted via ZsimDemo 3.30d software. The equivalent circuit diagram is shown in Fig. 7 , the R s and R ct are solution resistance and charge-transfer resistance, respectively. The L and R L correspond to the inductance and inductive resistance, respectively. The CPE denotes the constant phase element, which is substituted for the double-layer capacitance (C dl) on the surface of metal electrodes. The C dl can be defined as follows [63]:

The EIS parameter is listed in Table 1 . The R ct values are increased, and the C dl values are reduced with the increase in CPDs concentration. This phenomenon is attributed to the substitution of water molecules by CPD inhibitors molecules and adsorbed on the metal/solution interface to build up an adsorption layer [65]. The maximum values of η EIS reach 98.42 % when the CPD inhibitors concentration is 150 mg·L-1, which is higher than other CPD inhibitors, CPDs-160 for 93.23 %, CPDs-180 for 95.83 % and CPDs-200 for 96.09 %. Significantly, The CPDs-[PMIm]HSO4 is also an excellent corrosion inhibitor, the Nyquist and Bode plots are displayed in Fig. S20. The η EIS reaches 97.02 % (Table S3) when the CPDs-[PMIm]HSO4 inhibitor concentration is 150 mg·L-1. Meanwhile, at the lower inhibitor concentration (30 mg·L-1), the η EIS reach 67.55 %, lower than CPDs-220 for 82.81 %.

The sustainability of the corrosion inhibition performance of CPDs-220 inhibitor was investigated. The corrosion inhibition performance of CPDs for 45# steel in 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution for different immersion times was carried out. The Nyquist plots for 45# steel in 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution with 150 mg·L-1 CPDs-220 immersion for different times is shown in Fig. S11. The electrochemical parameter fitted data are listed in Table S2. The R ct value presents a decreasing trend with immersion time, which could be the consequence of the decreased desorption of the CPDs inhibitor from the metal surface. Significantly, the R ct value after 12 h of immersion is still kept higher than the blank solution, and the values of η EIS reach 97.33 %. The long immersion result demonstrates that CPD inhibitors provide superior long-term inhibition for 45# steel due to the tight adsorption of CPDs on the steel surface, limiting the metal dissolution.

The potentiodynamic polarization curves of 45# steel in 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution without and with four CPD inhibitors are depicted in Fig. 8 . It is found that introducing four CPD inhibitors and increasing concentration caused the cathodic and the anodic polarization curves to shift towards a lower current density direction, which means that the anodic iron dissolve reaction and cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction was inhibited. No significant change in the slope of the cathodic and the anodic polarization curves of four CPD inhibitors was observed, manifesting that the reaction mechanism remains unchanged on both the cathode and the anode with the addition of CPD inhibitors to the corrosive solution. The electrochemical parameters, including corrosion current density (i corr), the corrosion potential (E corr), and the cathodic Tafel slopes (β c) and anodic Tafel slopes (β a), were calculated from the Tafel extrapolation of the polarization curves and listed in Table 2 . and i corr stand for corrosion current density absence and presence of N, S-CPD inhibitors, respectively.

The E ocp can shift to a more negative value after the addition of CPDs, compared with the blank solution (Fig. S10). The deviation of E corr was smaller than ± 85 mV with the addition of four CPD inhibitors, which implies that four CPD inhibitors belong to mix-type inhibitors. The i corr has also shown noteworthy declines compared with the blank solution with the introduction of inhibitors, proving that CPDs can prevent corrosion reactions. The decrease in i corr was attributed to the adsorption of CPDs onto the 45# steel surface through functional groups containing N and S as active sites and the consequent formation of an adsorption film that impedes the corrosion reaction. The η Tafel values of various conditions CPDs with different temperatures were investigated to demonstrate the crucial role of the reaction temperature. As the reaction temperatures of the preparation CPDs increased, the i corr displayed a downward tendency, and the η Tafel values showed an upward trend, which means that the corrosion rate decreased. Simultaneously, the η Tafel was markedly improved with increasing CPDs concentration. When the dosage of CPDs-160 and CPDs-180, CPDs-200, and CPDs-220 was 150 mg·L-1, the η Tafel values were 93.23 %, 96.16 %, 96.58 %, and 99.14 %, respectively. For the Fig. S21 and Table S4, the PDP curves result showed that the CPDs-[PMIm]HSO4 belong to mix-type inhibitors, which mainly inhibited the cathodic reaction. While analyzing the η Tafel, the corrosion inhibition performance was the following: CPDs-220 > CPDs-[PMIm]HSO4.

Table S5 summarizes the corrosion inhibition efficiency of different N, S-rich CD inhibitors. The CPD inhibitors in this study, prepared in the presence of ionic liquid, have higher inhibition efficiency than other CD inhibitors, which can be attributed to the grafting of ionic liquids, and the resultant charged surface of CPDs reduces the agglomeration of carbon dots effectively.

The surface morphology of 45# steel in 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution without and with four inhibitors was illustrated in Fig. 9 . We can observe a smooth metal surface before the corrosion test. Many corrosion products and badly rough structures were watched, suggesting the 45# steel sample suffered corrosion damage in the blank solution. However, after the introduction of CPD inhibitors, the surface became smooth because of the inhibitor's adsorption on the metal surface. The surfaces of 45# steel soaked in CPDs-220 of 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution had the smoothest, exhibiting more robust anti-corrosion performance.

To demonstrate that CPDs could adsorb on the steel surface and form a protective layer, we examined the XPS spectrum for 45# steel in 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution in the presence of 150 mg·L-1 of CPDs-220. As shown in high-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. 10b ), the C 1 s band can be deconvoluted into four peaks. The peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.5 eV, 286 eV, and 288.2 eV were associated with CC/C = C, CN, C = N, and C = O bond, respectively [30,66]. In the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 10c), the binding energy peaks of Fe0, Fe2+, and Fe3+ were observed. The peaks at 706.7 eV were relevant to Fe0. The Fe 2p spectrum shows the presence of Fe3+ species, whose binging energy peaks at 713.0 eV and 726.6 eV. The peaks at 710.4 eV and 724.0 eV are assigned for Fe2+ 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 orbital. The Fe 2p spectrum contains binging energy peaks of Fe2+ and Fe3+, indicating the formation of iron corrosion products, including Fe2O3, FeOOH, or Fe3O4 species [31,67–69]. The O 1 s spectra (Fig. 10d) show three prominent peaks located at 529.9 eV, 531.4 eV, and 532.2 eV, which are ascribable to the O2- come from iron oxides and hydroxides, -OH of hydrous iron oxides and C = O, respectively [16,31]. The N 1 s spectra (Fig. 10e) are divided into three independent peaks, which correspond to CN, N-Fe, and N + H, at 399.4 eV, 400.4 eV, and 401.3 eV, respectively [37,70,71]. The S 2p spectra (Fig. 10f) exhibit two peaks, the peak at 168.5 eV and 169.7 eV is explained by the SO4 2- which adsorbs to the metal surface [30,38]. The XPS result confirmed the formation of adsorption film on the steel surface by multi-anchor adsorption model.

The electrochemical data has been fitted into four different isotherm models, including Langmuir, Freundlich, Frumkin, and El-Awady, to study the CPD inhibitor adsorption on the steel surface in the 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution. The R 2 value obtained from Langmuir isotherm model is significantly higher than other models, indicating that the adsorption process of CPD inhibitors can be effectively described by the Langmuir isotherm model. The fitting results of Freundlich, Frumkin and El-Awady isotherm models are displays in Fig. S13-S15, respectively.

The Langmuir isotherm equation is as follows [72]:
(6)
C inh
θ
=
C inh
+
1
K ads

Where, θ ,
C inh
, and
K ads
represents the surface coverage, inhibitor concentration and, adsorption equilibrium constant, respectively. As seen in Fig. 11 , the plot of C inh/θ vs. C inh displays a straight line with a correlation coefficient of four CPD inhibitors >0.99, showing that the adsorption of these inhibitors in H2SO4 solution is compatible with the Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The K ads values of CPDs-160, CPDs-180, CPDs-200, and CPDs-220 are 141.5, 162.6, 228.6, and 329.1 L·g-1, respectively. The K ads values decrease in the following order: CPDs-220 > CPDs-200 > CPDs-180> CPDs-160. Generally, the higher the K ads value, the more power the adsorption effect. Consequently, CPDs-220 exhibits the best inhibition performance.

The standard adsorption Gibbs free energy (
Δ

a d s
Θ
) is a vital parameter used to describe the adsorption of the inhibitor. The following formula calculates the standard adsorption Gibbs free energy [73].
(7)

Where R denotes the gas constant with a value of 8.314 J·(mol·K)−1, T represents the thermodynamic Kelvin temperature, and 1000 is the mass concentration of water. The adsorption is considered physisorption if the
Δ

a d s

between −40 kJ·mol-1 and −20 kJ·mol-1, the adsorption can be identified as mixed adsorption, including chemisorption and physisorption [74]. After calculation, the
Δ
G

Θ
values of CPDs-160, CPDs-180, CPDs-200, and CPDs-220 were −29.40 kJ·mol-1, −29.74 kJ·mol-1, −30.59 kJ·mol-1, and −31.49 kJ·mol-1, respectively. The
Δ

a d s

values for four CPD inhibitors are negative, suggesting that the adsorption process is spontaneous. The
Δ
G
a d s
Θ
values of this work synthesize CPD inhibitors with a range of −40 kJ·mol-1 and −20 kJ·mol-1, indicating that CPD inhibitors adsorbed on steel surfaces belong to mixed adsorption, including chemisorption and physisorption.

We used MD simulations technology to evaluate the interaction between studied CPDs-220 and Fe substrate, and the anti-corrosion mechanism was discussed. Fig. 12 describes the side and top perspectives of the equilibrium configuration for the adsorption of CPD inhibitors on the Fe (1 1 0) surface. As shown in Fig. 12. CPD inhibitors adsorbed on Fe surface with parallel orientation, where an interaction between inhibitors and Fe surfaces could form through physical and chemical adsorption. The binding energy E binding is 303.82 kJ·mol-1 for CPDs. The MD result showed that CPDs adsorbed onto metal surfaces with parallel orientation afforded a high coverage area to inhibit the corrosion reaction.

According to the adsorption isotherm analysis, the four CPD inhibitors existed in 45# steel surface surfaces, consisting of physical and chemical adsorption (Fig. 13 ). Firstly, the steel surfaces with a positive surface charge can attract the negatively charged SO4 2- from the H2SO4 solution, resulting in steel surfaces becoming negatively charged. Then, CPD inhibitors are protonated in an H2SO4 solution and exhibit a positive charge. The CPD inhibitors can absorb on steel surfaces and belong to physical adsorption, which is attributed to electrostatic interaction between CPDs with adsorbed (SO4 2-)ads ions. On the other hand, the chemical adsorption was ascribed to the functional group containing N and S elements of CPDs. The CPD inhibitors can donate electrons from lone pair electrons in a functional group of N and S elements of CPD inhibitors to the unoccupied d-orbital of Fe to form the coordination bond. In a blank H2SO4 solution, the corrosion reaction of 45# steel is manifested as iron dissolution of the anode reaction and hydrogen evolution of the cathode reaction. With the introduction of the CPD inhibitors, these can form a compact adsorption layer on steel surfaces to prevent corrosion, which simultaneous restraint of anodic and cathodic corrosion reactions of steel. The MD simulations show that the CPD inhibitors can be adsorbed on the Fe surface with parallel orientation, which means that CPD inhibitors provide a larger cover area on the surface to hinder corrosion.

The CPDs-220 prepared for 220 °C exhibits the best inhibition performance. Among the series of CPD inhibitors, CPDs-220 had the highest pyrrole-like N content (48.54 %) (Fig. S4b). This means the CPDs-220 has a more functional group of N and S elements to provide more adsorption active sites.

In this study, a series of CPD inhibitors were prepared by hydrothermal reaction and evaluated as corrosion inhibitors for 45# steel in 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4. The FT-IR and XPS show that the N, S-rich IL-grafted CPDs were synthesized successfully at different reaction temperatures. The ionic liquid is successfully grafted on the surface of CPDs. The electrochemical measure shows that four CPD inhibitors belong to mixed inhibitors and exhibit stronger protection ability for 45# steel in 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution. The η values obtained from the polarization test reached 94.07 % at 298 K with 50 mg·L-1 CPDs-220. The long-term immersion experiment results indicated that CPD inhibitors still protect 45# steel immersion in 0.5 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution for 12 h. The adsorption of CPDs on the 45# steel surface obeyed the Langmuir adsorption model, with both physisorption and chemisorption involved. The CPD inhibitors provide more adsorption sites and are adsorbed on the Fe surface with parallel orientation.
